title: Carved sycamore soup spoon
subtitle: A rounded, deep-bowled green-wood spoon for soups and stews
excerpt: A first hook-knife project for the home carver: a wide, rounded soup spoon in green sycamore. Shorter handle than a cooking spoon, rounder bowl, same axe-to-knife method. The round bowl shape means more hook-knife work and less axe work than most beginner spoons.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for spoon-blank proportions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the axe-to-knife roughing sequence.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving. 75 to 85 mm blade, scandi grind.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon or kuksa. The medium radius hook knife suits most everyday spoon bowls.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.

content.text: A soup spoon has a rounder, deeper bowl than a dessert spoon and a handle short enough to rest in the bowl without tipping the dish. The round shape makes it a good first hook-knife project: there is no long straight-sided oval to keep even, only a curved interior to hollow out to a consistent depth. The project uses
green wood
; fresh sycamore carved before it dries. Sycamore carves cleanly, dries food-safe and pale, and is widely available as coppice off-cuts, windfall, and pruning waste.
The wood
Source a fresh sycamore billet: 26 cm long, 8 cm across the widest face, 5 cm deep. The
blank

cut surface should feel slightly cool and damp, and show a glossy end-grain. Birch is a close alternative; similar working properties and similar food-safety record. Cherry works too but is harder to hollow cleanly with a hook knife when green.
Tools
A carving axe for roughing, a
sloyd knife
for profiling and the back of the bowl, a medium
hook knife
for the bowl interior, a leather strop, and raw linseed oil. Every edge must shave hair from the back of your forearm before you start.
Method
Use push cuts as the default throughout; blade away from the body, forearm driving the cut off the end of the workpiece into open air. Strop every 15 minutes.

Draw the spoon outline on the flat face: a 20 cm handle, a 7 cm round bowl. Mark the bowl depth on the side; the bowl floor sits 1.5 cm below the flat face. Keep the proportions generous; a smaller bowl is harder to hollow cleanly.

Rough the outline with the carving axe: stand the billet on the chopping block, axe down through the waste areas on either side of the handle, then trim the bowl outline. Work with the grain; ash and sycamore both have a visible straight grain; follow it. Stay 5 mm outside the pencil lines.

Refine the handle and neck with the sloyd knife, using push cuts along the handle and thumb-pivot cuts at the neck where the grain changes direction. The handle cross-section is oval, not round. Four-finger grip on the blank, thumb-push grip on the knife.

Hollow the bowl with the medium hook knife. Start at the centre and work in small arcs from the centre toward the rim; for a round bowl the grain runs in all directions relative to the cut, so short controlled arcs of 2 to 3 cm are safer than long strokes. The bowl depth is 1.5 cm at the centre. Check by inserting a knife point vertically at the deepest part and measuring against the outside face.

Shape the outside of the bowl with the sloyd knife: convex on the back, thinning toward the rim. Test wall thickness by pinching the rim between thumb and forefinger; 3 to 4 mm is right; you should feel resistance but the wall should not feel thick.
Leave to dry slowly, flat on a shelf away from direct heat, for 2 to 3 weeks. Sand through 120 and 180 grit once fully dry.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil to the dry, sanded spoon. Let it soak in for an hour, wipe off the surplus, and leave to cure for 2 weeks before use. The spoon is not food-safe until the oil has fully cured. Lay oiled cloths flat to dry; never leave them bundled, as they can smoulder.
Variations
Birch: similar working properties, slightly lighter weight. Good where sycamore is not available fresh.
Left-handed version: angle the bowl slightly left at the neck after the roughing stage.
Dessert spoon: the same shape but 22 cm total length and a 5 cm bowl.
Care
Wash promptly after use, towel-dry, never soak or dishwash. Re-oil when the wood looks pale and dry, roughly every 4 to 6 weeks in regular use.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems and fixes for the soup spoon.

items.symptom: Bowl interior is lumpy and uneven
items.cause: Hook knife arcs too long, riding over the high spots rather than cutting them
items.fix: Shorten the arc to 2 cm. Feel the interior with a fingertip after each pass to identify the high spots, then aim the next arc directly at them.

items.cause: Neck walls too thick and uneven; one face dries faster than the other
items.fix: Carve the neck to an even 3 to 4 mm before leaving to dry. Equalise the wall thickness on all four faces.
Strop the hook knife on the concave side of the leather strop, pulling the edge along it. Three passes usually restores the bite.
